results.title: Studying the Benefits of Active Recall in learning Physics Formulas at Young Achievers’ School
excerpts:
A quasi-experimental research design was employed which involves 30 respondents chosen through simple random sampling. The participants were divided into two groups: the experimental group, which used active recall, and the control group, which depended on traditional memorization. Pre-test and Posttest were given to both groups and then the survey was conducted using Likert scale questionnaire made by the researchers. The findings showed that although both groups improved, the experimental group’s mean score (8.33) was greater than the control group (4.13). The null hypothesis was rejected since the computed t-value of 2.23 was greater than the critical value of 2.048 at 0.05 level of significance. This suggests that students' learning outcomes were significantly improved by active recall.
The survey also revealed that while some students were unsure about the long-term retention advantages of active recall, the majority of students had a positive view of it and were willing to use and suggest it as a study method. The study determined that active recall improves physics formulas retention, comprehension and its use more than traditional memorization.

## Citations (0) ## References (9)

ResearchGate has not been able to resolve any citations for this publication.

Impact of an active learning physics workshop on secondary school students’ self-efficacy and ability

Article

Full-text available

* Oct 2020

* [Jessie Durk](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Jessie-Durk)
* [Ally Davies](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Ally-Davies)
* [Robin Hughes](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Robin-Hughes-2184459266)
* [Lisa Jardine-Wright](https://www.researchgate.net/scientific-contributions/Lisa-Jardine-Wright-2171558012)

Female students and those with a low socioeconomic status (SES) typically score lower in assessments of self-efficacy and ability in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM). In this study, a cohort of over 200 UK students attended an intensive, active learning, physics workshop, with pre- and postassessments to measure both physics self-efficacy and physics ability before and after the workshop. Our control took the form of material that was closely related but not covered during the workshop. Students benefited from attending the workshop, as self-efficacy and ability increased significantly in the post-test, with the material not covered showing the smallest increase as expected.

results.title: 35 million tons of food go to waste yearly in the US - ABC15 Arizona
excerpts:
[News](https://www.abc15.com/news) [National News](https://www.abc15.com/news/national)

## Actions

* [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/dialog/share?app_id=1561178210822538&display=popup&href=https://www.abc15.com/news/national/35-million-tons-of-food-go-to-waste-yearly-in-the-us-experts-share-tips-to-help-stop-it)
* [Tweet](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.abc15.com/news/national/35-million-tons-of-food-go-to-waste-yearly-in-the-us-experts-share-tips-to-help-stop-it&text=35%20million%20tons%20of%20food%20go%20to%20waste%20yearly%20in%20the%20US.%20Experts%20share%20tips%20to%20help%20stop%20it)
* Email

# 35 million tons of food go to waste yearly in the US. Experts share tips to help stop it

On Thanksgiving alone, ReFED estimated that 320 million pounds of food— $550 million worth— was thrown away in a single day

Prev Next

Millions of tons of food are wasted each year in the United States alone.
ABC15 Meteorologist Ashlee DeMartino has some tips for how you can reduce how much food you are throwing out this holiday season.

By: CNN

Posted

and last updated

Millions of tons of food are wasted each year in the United States alone.

About 35 million tons, to be specific, according to the [latest ReFED report](https://refed.org/uploads/consumer-food-waste-report-2025-final.pdf) . Some [31%](https://refed.org/food-waste/the-problem/) of food that is grown and produced goes unsold or uneaten in the US, estimates ReFED, a nonprofit organization focused on reducing food waste.

Half of all the food waste comes from consumers. “That’s either groceries — the strawberries that spoil in your fridge — or the meal you ordered at the restaurant and only ate half of or didn’t eat the leftovers when you brought them home,” said Sara Burnett, executive director of ReFED.
That waste wreaks havoc on our planet, she said, noting that 35 million tons of food waste “is equivalent to the greenhouse gas emission of 154 million metric tons of carbon, which is about the same as driving 36 million passenger cars for a year, and it consumes 9 trillion gallons of water, which is about 13 million Olympic-sized pools.”

On Thanksgiving alone, ReFED estimated that [320 million pounds of food](https://refed.org/articles/how-much-food-will-go-to-waste-this-thanksgiving-about-550-million-worth/) — $550 million worth— was thrown away in a single day.

“The holidays are a time of gathering, celebration, and abundance, and with that comes an unintended consequence—food waste,” Burnett said. “Thanksgiving—the biggest food waste day of the year—kicks off a season where some estimates say household waste increases by 25%, and that includes a lot of food.

“Fortunately, there are easy and practical ways to reduce waste in your kitchen and save money in the process!”

results.title: Spaced Repetition: The Key to Long-Term Memory (2026) | Cramd Blog
excerpts:
But if you wait too long between sessions, the pathway starts to weaken - and you're basically starting from scratch.

Spaced repetition catches you right at the moment of near-forgetting. That moment of effortful retrieval is exactly what cements information into long-term memory. The harder the recall, the stronger the memory that follows.

### What Is the Forgetting Curve?

The forgetting curve is a concept introduced by psychologist Hermann Ebbinghaus in the 1880s - and [later replicated by modern researchers](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4492928/) \- that maps how quickly memories decay without reinforcement.

The curve drops fast. After just one day, a huge chunk of what you learned is already gone. Without review, that curve keeps falling. Spaced repetition is essentially a series of strategically timed "saves" that flatten that curve and keep information accessible long-term.

* * *

## The Science Behind Spaced Repetition and Active Recall Studying
This isn't just study hack folklore. The research is extensive, consistent, and spans decades.

### Why Cramming Fails

Cramming feels productive because you're spending a lot of time with the material. But passive re-reading and last-minute review sessions don't actually build durable memories - they just create a temporary illusion of familiarity.

[Research on active recall studying](https://mics.edu.gh/active-recall-vs-passive-review-which-works-better-for-students/) shows a striking gap: students who actively tested themselves retained around 80% of material, compared to just 30% for those who passively reviewed. Despite this, [studies show that over 90% of students still default to passive re-reading](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S187712972500231X) as their primary study method.

### How Spaced Repetition Rewires Your Memory

Information moves through three stages before it sticks:

1. **Initial learning** \- New material enters short-term memory.
Without reinforcement, it fades fast.
2. **Consolidation** \- Strategic review at the right intervals transfers knowledge into long-term memory.
3. **Retention** \- Over time, spaced repetition strengthens neural connections to the point where recall becomes automatic.

[A 2025 review published in PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39250798/) confirmed that spaced repetition consistently outperforms massed (cramming) study for long-term retention across different learner types and subjects. It's not a niche technique - it's the most evidence-backed approach to memorization available.

Pair this with [active recall](https://trycramd.com/blog/power-of-active-recall) \- the practice of retrieving information from memory rather than passively reviewing it - and you've got arguably the most powerful study combination in existence.

* * *

## How Cramd's Spaced Repetition App Works

Knowing that spaced repetition works is one thing.

results.title: The Science Behind Active Recall | Studley AI
excerpts:
Active recall is a learning technique based on retrieving information from memory rather than simply re-reading or re-watching content. When you try to recall a fact, concept, or explanation from memory (even unsuccessfully), you are actively engaging your brain in a way that strengthens the associated neural connections.

Dr. Sarah Martinez, lead neuroscientist at MIT's Learning Lab, explains:

> "When you actively retrieve information instead of passively consuming it, you're literally rewiring your brain. Each retrieval attempt strengthens the memory pathway, making future recall faster and more reliable."

Unlike passive study methods like highlighting, rereading, or watching lecture videos, active recall demands that your brain do the work of remembering — and **that mental effort is what builds memory strength** .

Some examples of active recall in practice:

* Answering practice questions from memory
* Covering up notes and trying to write them out again
* Teaching a topic aloud without looking at your materials
* Using flashcards and recalling answers before flipping
* Doing self-quizzes and practice tests

## The Proven Power of Active Recall

A meta-analysis from the University of Toronto in 2024 analyzed **217 peer-reviewed studies** on learning and memory. Their findings:

* Students using active recall scored **73% higher on delayed retention tests**
* Test scores improved by an average of **38%** compared to traditional methods
* Active recall users were **2\.4x more likely** to remember content during stressful exams
* Learners identified **92% more knowledge gaps** during study sessions
* Metacognitive awareness increased by **64%** , allowing students to study more strategically

In short, **active recall improves memory, comprehension, critical thinking, and confidence** .

## The Neuroscience Behind It

Using advanced fMRI technology, Stanford’s Memory Lab found that active recall activates multiple brain regions at once:
* ▶ **Prefrontal Cortex** : responsible for decision-making and focused thinking
* ▶ **Hippocampus** : crucial for forming and organizing new memories
* ▶ **Temporal Lobe** : processes language and integrates sensory input

This distributed brain activity is what makes active recall so powerful. The effort of retrieving information sends a stronger signal through the brain’s memory circuits, leading to **longer-lasting retention and deeper understanding** .

## Why Passive Study Fails (And Students Don't Realize It)

The illusion of competence is a major trap. When students re-read notes or watch videos, they often feel like they understand the material simply because it's familiar. But this is short-term recognition, not true understanding or retention.

Passive study techniques like:

* Highlighting textbooks
* Rewatching lectures
* Copying notes verbatim

...may feel productive but do **very little to improve test performance or long-term memory** .

results.title: The global unbanked shoppers
excerpts:
* BankingTech
* Commentary

# The global unbanked shoppers

The gap between the population of the banked and unbanked is continuing to broaden.

FinTech Futures

March 5, 2020

4 Min Read

[](https://www.linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite/?url=https://www.fintechfutures.com/bankingtech/the-global-unbanked-shoppers) [](http://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.fintechfutures.com/bankingtech/the-global-unbanked-shoppers) [](http://www.twitter.com/intent/tweet?url=https://www.fintechfutures.com/bankingtech/the-global-unbanked-shoppers) [](https://www.reddit.com/submit?url=https://www.fintechfutures.com/bankingtech/the-global-unbanked-shoppers&title=The%20global%20unbanked%20shoppers) [](https://bsky.app/intent/compose?text=The%20global%20unbanked%20shoppers%20-%20https%3A%2F%2Fwww.fintechfutures.com%2Fbankingtech%2Fthe-global-unbanked-shoppers)

There is an assumption that everyone today has a bank account.
But this isn’t always the case; according to the latest report from World Bank Group, as many as 1.7 billion adults are unbanked globally. That’s roughly one-fourth of the global population, explores James Booth, VP, head of payment partnerships EMEA at PPRO.

The report indicates something most people would expect, most unbanked adults live in areas of the developing world, such as Bangladesh, China, India, Mexico, Nigeria and Pakistan. However, less predictably, the main factor for large portions of people remaining unbanked in these countries isn’t necessarily down to their income level.

Of these unbanked adults, only half come from the poorest 40% of households within their economy. A lack of education and unemployment are only a small part of the picture; the cost of accounts and distance to the nearest bank are also contributing factors.

In Europe, countries such as Denmark, Finland, Netherlands, Norway and Sweden have a 100% banked population.
But, like any aspect of culture, the population of banked consumers can vary significantly from country to country, and they do in Europe. Take Hungary, for example, with a 75% banked population and the Czech Republic at 81%.

The unbanked shopping experience

In 2020, commerce is customer centric. At a time when any device can become a point of sale, consumers expect a seamless, integrated shopping experience. The shopping habits of consumers – including the unbanked ones – must be taken into account when optimising their experience. A big part of that is consumers’ preferred methods of payment. Take the UK, for example. The number of contactless payments made in the UK surged by 31% in a year to reach 7.4bn in 2018, highlighting that the UK remains one of the highest users of debit and credit-based payment methods. However, this is not the case for the rest of the world.

results.title: 911 call takers play a crucial role in deciding when to send police alternatives : NPR
excerpts:
org/sections/health-shots/2023/11/09/1203342875/cities-know-the-way-police-respond-to-mental-crisis-calls-needs-to-change-but-ho)

As more cities deploy these new responders, the decision of when to use them usually rests with already stressed 911 workers.

Harper found her job as a 911 call taker very rewarding. She felt like she was really helping people. But she often worked long hours and struggled with anxiety. After a few years, she decided it was in her best interest to put the headset down.

That experience isn't unusual, says Jessica Gillooly, an assistant professor of sociology and criminal justice at Suffolk University.

[Enlarge this image](https://media.npr.org/assets/img/2024/04/04/imadali-denver911-41_custom-2e4b9a1b69859c66e444f1de12ea07cacbeff7ca.jpg?s=2600&c=100&f=jpeg)

Linda Anderson, an emergency communications technician, during a call at the Denver 911 dispatch center. **Eli Imadali for NPR** ****hide caption****

****toggle caption****

Eli Imadali for NPR
Linda Anderson, an emergency communications technician, during a call at the Denver 911 dispatch center.

Eli Imadali for NPR

"Nationally, I'd say that right now it's not a great time in the 911 call taking system. Some might say it's in crisis itself," Gillooly says. "Workers are overstressed, they're understaffed and they're underpaid."

In a [survey of 911 professionals](https://cdn.ymaws.com/www.nena.org/resource/resmgr/docs/2023_Carbyne_and_NENA_The_Pu.pdf) last year, more than 80 percent of respondents said their facility was understaffed. Almost the same amount said they regularly deal with burnout and anxiety.

"You are the voice amidst the chaos, but you're not seen. And because you are not on scene, you're not uniformed, you're not interacting with someone at the location, you're forgotten about," says Carlena Orosco, an assistant professor of criminal justice at California State University, Los Angeles, who spent nine years as a dispatcher.
"You're considered someone who gets resources, handles things, gets officers to the scene, and then you're kind of left out of the picture at that point," she added, "without having any closure or resolution or even being treated as though you're a key decision maker, which you are."

Sponsor Message

### 'We have to make sure that people are on the same page'

[Enlarge this image](https://media.npr.org/assets/img/2024/04/04/imadali-denver911-28_custom-ea3aa47c8689f8d4263d4e44591267d16ab0a6ea.jpg?s=2600&c=100&f=jpeg)

Silvia Morales, a bilingual emergency communications technician, works between calls at Denver 911. **Eli Imadali for NPR** ****hide caption****

****toggle caption****

Eli Imadali for NPR

Silvia Morales, a bilingual emergency communications technician, works between calls at Denver 911.

Eli Imadali for NPR

Deciding what might be a good fit for an alternative response can be challenging, Gillooly says.

"It can be hard to understand what's going on in the moment.

results.title: Active Recall: The Most Effective High-Yield Learning Technique - Osmosis Blog
excerpts:
si=ksZJHKZoFSywSQzP) [n](https://www.osmosis.org/blog/learning-how-to-use-spaced-repetition-in-medical-school) into your topics and questions is another scientifically validated way to boost memory retention. Online question-based resources such as Anki and Osmosis flashcards can also lend a helping hand. Have a look around and see what works for you!

## What are the cons of active recall?

So, we’ve covered the pros of active recall; what are the downsides? I can sum up the cons in one sentence: **active recall is hard work** . Continually testing yourself requires significant cognitive effort compared to studying by simply reading and highlighting your notes. It can be disheartening when you are struggling to answer questions on a difficult topic, and the temptation to simply read your notes instead can be very strong. But do your best to persevere and thank yourself later!

## Conclusion
Active recall is the **most efficient, high-yield study technique** that involves repeatedly testing yourself using questions created from your notes. A **wealth of scientific research** proves the efficacy of active recall in significantly boosting memory retention and test performance when compared to passively re-reading and highlighting notes. Incorporating active recall into your study methodology has the potential to maximize your learning efficiency and ace those pesky exams. Well, what are you waiting for?

## Key Takeaways

* Active recall improves memory by actively retrieving information, not just rereading.
* Practice testing helps identify knowledge gaps for targeted studying.
* Using spaced repetition boosts long-term retention.
* Active recall requires effort but leads to better exam results.
* Combining active recall with tools like flashcards enhances learning efficiency.

## References

* Butler, A. C. (2010).
[Repeated testing produces superior transfer of learning relative to repeated studying](https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2010-17631-003) . _Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition_ , _36_ (5), 1118.
* Carrier, L. M. (2003). [College students’ choices of study strategies](https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2003-03926-008) . _Perceptual and Motor skills_ , _96_ (1), 54-56.
* Dunlosky, J., Rawson, K. A., Marsh, E. J., Nathan, M. J., & Willingham, D. T. (2013). [Improving students’ learning with effective learning techniques: Promising directions from cognitive and educational psychology](https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2013-00960-002) . _Psychological Science in the Public Interest_ , _14_ (1), 4-58.
* Karpicke, J. D., & Blunt, J. R. (2011). [Retrieval practice produces more learning than elaborative studying with concept mapping](https://psycnet.apa.org/record/2011-04504-004) . _Science_ , _331_ (6018), 772-775.
* Karpicke, J. D., Butler, A. C., & Roediger III, H.

# Survey: More Than Half of 911 Centers Face Staffing Crisis

Published Apr 2023

The staffing crisis within emergency communications is not new, but there’s reason to believe the problem is worsening. A study released by the [International Academies of Dispatch (IAED)](https://www.emergencydispatch.org/home) and the [National Association of State 911 Administrators (NASNA)](https://www.nasna911.org/home) reports that more than half of 911 centers in the U.S. are facing a genuine staffing emergency.

The survey of 774 PSAPs/ECCs in 47 U.S. states found that nearly one-third of respondents reported high vacancy rates in 2022, and nearly every center said they’d lost employees last year, for a total of 3,952 staff departures across the centers surveyed.
**Other highlights from the survey:**

* The staffing crisis appears to affect centers of all sizes and across every region and parts of every state surveyed.
* Vacancy rates varied, but nearly every center experienced some unfilled positions: 166 centers reported a vacancy rate ranging from 30% to 49%; 92 reported a vacancy rate between 50% and 69%; and 13 said 70% or more of their positions were unfilled.
* More than one-third of centers said they have fewer positions filled in 2022 than in 2019, in spite of an increasing volume of emergency calls annually.

The [survey](https://www.emergencydispatch.org/in-the-news/press-releases/64632133-7f9f-4d77-8013-d59c445fdb88) , conducted jointly by IAED and NASNA, was released at NENA’s [“911 Goes to Washington”](https://www.nena.org/page/gtw) event in Washington, DC, in February. Watch for the full report to be released later this year.

results.title: Global Unbanked Population 2026: 1.5 Billion Adults Without Banks
excerpts:
44% of the world’s adults are still using cash to make at least one of their utility payments each month, and the largest group of unbanked adults reside in Asia Pacific - due sheerly to large population sizes in rural Asia, despite the largest portion of unbanked people reside in Africa. While there are 493 million unbanked adults in the Middle East and Africa, there are nearly twice that many - or 820 million unbanked adults in APAC. APAC also houses the most underbanked adults with 1.8 billion people living and operating without a credit card.

## Regional Breakdown: Where the Unbanked Reside

While the highest _percentage_ of unbanked individuals is found in the Middle East and Africa, the highest _raw volume_ is concentrated in the Asia-Pacific (APAC) region due to its massive population density.

* **Asia-Pacific (APAC):** Houses the largest unbanked population with **820 million adults** . It also leads the "underbanked" category, with **1\.
78 billion people** operating without a credit card.
* **Middle East & Africa (MEA):** Has the highest concentration of financial exclusion, with **52% of adults** (493 million) remaining unbanked.
* **Latin America:** Shows a significant reliance on physical currency, with **60% cash usage** —the highest of any major global region.

## The Mobile-First Opportunity

There is a profound disconnect between digital connectivity and financial inclusion. Globally, **89% of adults own a mobile phone** , yet only a fraction utilize that device for formal banking. This "mobile-first" landscape represents a multi-billion dollar opportunity for fintechs to bridge the gap in regions like Europe and North America, where mobile penetration sits at 96% and 93% respectively.

* * *

### Global Financial Inclusion Stats by Region

#### 2026 Regional Comparison: Unbanked vs. Underbanked

|**Region** |**Unbanked (%)** |**Unbanked (M)** |**Cash Usage (%)** |**Mobile Penetration** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|**World Total** |**27%** |**1,546M** |**44%** |**89%** |
|Asia-Pacific |25% |820M |59% |85% |
|Middle East & Africa |52% |493M |58% |83% |
|Latin America |35% |164M |60% |87% |
|Europe |8% |54M |23% |96% |
|North America |5% |15M |21% |93% |

## FAQ

**How many people are unbanked in the world in 2026?**

According to the World Bank, approximately **1\.54 billion adults** worldwide remain unbanked. This accounts for **27%** of the global adult population.

**What is the difference between unbanked and underbanked?**

**Unbanked** individuals lack any account at a formal financial institution. **Underbanked** individuals (affecting 2.82 billion people) may have a basic account but lack access to essential financial tools like credit cards or insurance.

**Which region has the most unbanked adults?**

By raw numbers, the **Asia-Pacific (APAC)** region has the most unbanked adults with **820 million** .
